content.text: selecting the most relevant features from a dataset

to use when building ML models by identifying important features that contribute to predictive power and removing irrelevant or redundant ones, thereby improving model accuracy and reducing overfitting.
"While feature engineering creates new features, feature selection identifies the most important ones. It removes irrelevant or redundant features to improve efficiency and reduce model complexity."
Removing irrelevant features prevents model from learning noise
Model focuses only on what truly matters
Reduces irrelevant information that degrades performance
Learns simpler models with fewer features
: "In machine learning, better features lead to better models. Feature engineering transforms data. Feature selection refines it."
These are complementary but distinct steps:
Transform raw data into meaningful variables
Three main types of feature selection methods
statistical techniques to score each feature independently

:
- Score each feature based on statistical relationship with target
- Select top-N features or features above threshold
-

: Tests independence between feature and target
-

:
- Fast and simple
- Independent of model choice

:
- Ignores feature interactions
- May miss complex relationships
: Quick initial filtering, large datasets, exploratory analysis.
feature subsets based on model performance

:
- Try different combinations of features
- Train model on each combination
- Select combination with best performance

: Start with no features, add best one at each step
-
: Start with all features, remove worst one at each step

:
- Accounts for feature interactions
- Model-aware (finds best features for specific model)

(many model training runs)
- Can overfit to training data
: Smaller datasets, when you need optimal subset for specific model.

:
- Model learns which features are important
- Uses built-in mechanisms to select features

: Shrinks coefficients, zeros out unimportant features
-

: Faster than wrapper, more accurate than filter
- Model-aware (finds features for specific model)
- Efficient (single training run)

:
- Only works with models that support it
: Most common choice for production, good balance of speed and accuracy.

from sklearn.feature_selection import correlation_matrix

# Remove features with low correlation to target
correlations = df.corr()['target'].abs()
selected_features = correlations[correlations > 0.1].index

from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

model = RandomForestClassifier()
model.fit(X, y)

# Get feature importance
importances = model.feature_importances_
: Identify important features using model's built-in mechanism.

from sklearn.feature_selection import RFE

model = RandomForestClassifier()
rfe = RFE(estimator=model, n_features_to_select=10)
rfe.fit(X, y)

selected_features = X.columns[rfe.get_support()]
: Optimize feature subset for best performance.
Quick initial filter to remove obviously irrelevant features.
Best balance of speed and accuracy; Random Forest, Lasso work well.
Feature selection is iterative: select → train → evaluate → refine.
Features with correlation > 0.9 are likely redundant; remove one.

Filter methods score features independently using statistical measures of association with the target variable. For a continuous target and continuous feature, the Pearson correlation coefficient is the standard measure:
For categorical targets, mutual information measures how much knowing a feature reduces uncertainty about the class label:

attrs.latex: I(X; Y) = \sum_{x \in X} \sum_{y \in Y} P(x, y) \log \frac{P(x, y)}{P(x) P(y)}

content.text: Wrapper methods use model performance as their scoring criterion. Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) removes the least important feature at each step, training the model on the reduced set each time:
Start with all d features. Train model, get feature importances w₁, …, w_d.
Remove the feature with the lowest importance score.
Re-train on the remaining d−1 features. Repeat until the desired number of features remains.
Select the subset that maximised cross-validation performance during elimination.

Embedded methods leverage the model's own regularisation. L1 (Lasso) regression adds a penalty proportional to the absolute value of coefficients, which drives irrelevant coefficients exactly to zero:

The L1 penalty creates a sparse solution: features whose coefficients are zeroed out are effectively excluded. The regularisation strength λ is a hyperparameter that controls how aggressively features are removed — larger λ produces fewer selected features.
Real-World Example: Credit Risk Feature Selection

A credit scoring model might start with 200 raw features (payment history, balances, account ages, inquiry counts, etc.). Feature selection reduces this to the 20–30 most predictive variables, improving both model performance and regulatory explainability — regulations like GDPR require that automated decisions can be explained in terms of the input variables used.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV
from sklearn.feature_selection import (
    SelectKBest, mutual_info_classif,
    RFE, SelectFromModel
)
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Simulate credit scoring dataset: 1000 samples, 50 features, 10 informative
X, y = make_classification(
    n_samples=1000, n_features=50, n_informative=10,
    n_redundant=5, n_repeated=0, random_state=42
)
feature_names = [f"feat_{i}" for i in range(50)]

# ── Step 1: Filter — mutual information ──
selector_filter = SelectKBest(mutual_info_classif, k=20)
X_filter = selector_filter.fit_transform(X, y)
mi_scores = selector_filter.scores_
top_mi = np.argsort(mi_scores)[-10:][::-1]
print("Top 10 by mutual information:", [feature_names[i] for i in top_mi])

# ── Step 2: Embedded — Lasso (L1 regularised logistic regression) ──
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegressionCV
lasso_model = LogisticRegressionCV(
    Cs=10, penalty="l1", solver="saga",
    cv=5, max_iter=5000, random_state=42
)
lasso_model.fit(X, y)
nonzero_coefs = np.where(lasso_model.coef_[0] != 0)[0]
print(f"Lasso selected {len(nonzero_coefs)} features: {[feature_names[i] for i in nonzero_coefs]}")

# ── Step 3: Wrapper — RFE with Random Forest ──
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rfe = RFE(estimator=rf, n_features_to_select=15, step=1)
rfe.fit(X, y)
rfe_selected = np.where(rfe.support_)[0]
print(f"RFE selected: {[feature_names[i] for i in rfe_selected]}")

# ── Compare model performance ──
rf_all   = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rf_rfe   = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)

score_all = cross_val_score(rf_all, X, y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc").mean()
score_rfe = cross_val_score(rf_rfe, X[:, rfe_selected], y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc").mean()

print(f"AUC with all 50 features: {score_all:.4f}")
print(f"AUC with 15 RFE features: {score_rfe:.4f}")
# Often the reduced model matches or exceeds the full model due to less noise
Mistake 1: Feature selection before train-test split (data leakage)

# WRONG — selects features using the entire dataset, including test data
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=10)
X_selected = selector.fit_transform(X, y)              # sees all y labels!
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X_selected, y)

# RIGHT — select features only on training data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)
selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=10)
X_train_sel = selector.fit_transform(X_train, y_train)   # fit on train only
X_test_sel  = selector.transform(X_test)                  # apply to test
Mistake 2: Removing correlated features without checking which to keep

# WRONG — arbitrarily removes the first of each correlated pair
corr_matrix = pd.DataFrame(X).corr().abs()
upper = corr_matrix.where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
to_drop = [col for col in upper.columns if any(upper[col] > 0.9)]
# This ignores which of the two correlated features is more predictive

# RIGHT — keep the one with higher correlation to target
import pandas as pd, numpy as np
df = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=feature_names)
df["target"] = y
corr_with_target = df.corr()["target"].abs()
corr_matrix = df.drop("target", axis=1).corr().abs()
# Remove the feature with LOWER target correlation from each high-correlation pair
upper = corr_matrix.where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
to_drop = []
for col in upper.columns:
    for row in upper.index:
        if upper.loc[row, col] > 0.9:
            # Drop the one with lower target correlation
            if corr_with_target[col] < corr_with_target[row]:
                to_drop.append(col)
            else:
                to_drop.append(row)
to_drop = list(set(to_drop))
print("Dropping:", to_drop)

Filter methods run in O(n·d) time (n samples, d features) and are independent of the downstream model, making them suitable for datasets with thousands of features. Mutual information estimation for continuous features requires density estimation (or binning), which adds an O(n log n) term per feature but is still fast in absolute terms.

Wrapper methods are the bottleneck: RFE trains the model O(d) times, so total cost is O(d × model training time). For a dataset with 1000 features and a training time of 10 seconds per model, RFE takes 2.8 hours. Practical workarounds include: using a faster surrogate model (e.g., a shallow decision tree) for elimination and then retraining the full model on the selected features, or using step>1 to eliminate multiple features per round.

Embedded methods (Lasso, tree importance) add negligible overhead to a single model training run, making them the most scalable option for high-dimensional data. Random Forest feature importance is computed for free during training, with no additional computation cost.
— The standard library. Provides SelectKBest, SelectPercentile, RFE, RFECV, SelectFromModel, VarianceThreshold, chi2, f_classif, mutual_info_classif, and more.
— Model-agnostic feature importance using Shapley values. More reliable than tree-based importance for correlated features, but slower (O(n × features × samples)).
— A wrapper method that compares each feature against randomly permuted shadow features. More principled than RFE for determining which features are genuinely informative.
— Automated feature selection pipeline that combines correlation analysis, target correlation, and XGBoost importance with recursive elimination.

# SHAP-based feature importance (model-agnostic)
import shap
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier

model = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_train)

# Mean absolute SHAP value per feature
mean_shap = np.abs(shap_values).mean(axis=0)
top_features = np.argsort(mean_shap)[-10:][::-1]
print("Top 10 SHAP features:", [feature_names[i] for i in top_features])

# Plot (requires matplotlib)
# shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_train, feature_names=feature_names)

Feature selection decisions made on one dataset may not generalise to another. A common reliability failure is selecting features that are spuriously correlated with the label in the training sample due to sampling noise — particularly when the number of features d exceeds the number of samples n. This is the multiple comparisons problem: if you test 1000 features for correlation with a random label at p < 0.05, you expect 50 false positives.

Mitigation strategies: use cross-validated feature selection (RFECV rather than RFE) so that the selected set is stable across folds; apply Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg correction when using statistical tests; and test the selected feature set on a fully held-out test set that was never used during any stage of selection. Adversarial inputs that manipulate feature values to cross selection thresholds are a concern in high-stakes settings such as fraud detection — features selected purely on historical data may be gamed by adversaries who understand the model.

The following is a production-ready feature selection pipeline that combines all three method families — filter, embedded, and wrapper — in a systematic sequence. The pipeline is structured to minimise computation: cheap filter methods run first and eliminate the most obviously irrelevant features, reducing the cost of the more expensive embedded and wrapper stages.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegressionCV
from sklearn.feature_selection import (
    VarianceThreshold, SelectKBest, mutual_info_classif,
    RFE, RFECV, SelectFromModel
)
from sklearn.model_selection import StratifiedKFold, cross_val_score
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score

# ── 1. Generate realistic high-dimensional dataset ──
X, y = make_classification(
    n_samples=2000, n_features=100,
    n_informative=15, n_redundant=10,
    n_repeated=5, n_classes=2,
    random_state=42, flip_y=0.05
)
feature_names = [f"f{i:03d}" for i in range(100)]
print(f"Initial: {X.shape[1]} features")

# ── 2. Remove zero-variance features ──
vt = VarianceThreshold(threshold=0.01)
X_vt = vt.fit_transform(X)
feature_names_vt = [feature_names[i] for i in vt.get_support(indices=True)]
print(f"After variance threshold: {X_vt.shape[1]} features")

# ── 3. Remove highly correlated features (r > 0.95) ──
df = pd.DataFrame(X_vt, columns=feature_names_vt)
corr_matrix = df.corr().abs()
upper = corr_matrix.where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
to_drop_corr = {col for col in upper.columns if any(upper[col] > 0.95)}
df_uncorr = df.drop(columns=list(to_drop_corr))
X_uncorr = df_uncorr.values
feature_names_uncorr = list(df_uncorr.columns)
print(f"After correlation removal: {X_uncorr.shape[1]} features")

# ── 4. Filter: mutual information top-50% ──
k_filter = max(10, X_uncorr.shape[1] // 2)
mi_selector = SelectKBest(mutual_info_classif, k=k_filter)
X_mi = mi_selector.fit_transform(X_uncorr, y)
feature_names_mi = [feature_names_uncorr[i] for i in mi_selector.get_support(indices=True)]
print(f"After MI filter (k={k_filter}): {X_mi.shape[1]} features")

# ── 5. Embedded: Lasso (L1 logistic) ──
lasso = LogisticRegressionCV(
    Cs=np.logspace(-3, 1, 10), penalty="l1",
    solver="saga", cv=5, max_iter=3000, random_state=42
)
lasso.fit(X_mi, y)
lasso_mask = lasso.coef_[0] != 0
X_lasso = X_mi[:, lasso_mask]
feature_names_lasso = [feature_names_mi[i] for i, keep in enumerate(lasso_mask) if keep]
print(f"After Lasso embedded: {X_lasso.shape[1]} features")

# ── 6. Wrapper: RFECV (cross-validated RFE) ──
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
cv = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)
rfecv = RFECV(estimator=rf, step=1, cv=cv, scoring="roc_auc", min_features_to_select=5)
rfecv.fit(X_lasso, y)
X_final = X_lasso[:, rfecv.support_]
feature_names_final = [feature_names_lasso[i] for i, keep in enumerate(rfecv.support_) if keep]
print(f"After RFECV: {X_final.shape[1]} features (optimal)")

# ── 7. Compare performance ──
rf_all   = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rf_final = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)

auc_all   = cross_val_score(rf_all,   X,       y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc").mean()
auc_final = cross_val_score(rf_final, X_final, y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc").mean()

print(f"\nAUC (100 features): {auc_all:.4f}")
print(f"AUC ({X_final.shape[1]} features): {auc_final:.4f}")
print(f"Selected features: {feature_names_final}")
Statistical Tests Used in Filter Methods
Filter methods rely on statistical tests to score feature-target associations. The chi-squared test for independence is used when both the feature and target are categorical:

where O_i is the observed count and E_i is the expected count under independence. A high chi-squared value indicates the feature and target are not independent — i.e., the feature is informative about the class label.
ANOVA F-statistic is used for continuous features with a categorical target. It compares the variance between group means to the variance within groups:

attrs.latex: F = \frac{\text{between-group variance}}{\text{within-group variance}} = \frac{\sum_{k} n_k (\bar{x}_k - \bar{x})^2 / (K-1)}{\sum_{k} \sum_{i \in k} (x_i - \bar{x}_k)^2 / (n - K)}

content.text: A feature with high F-statistic has very different means across classes and is likely informative. Mutual information I(X; Y) — covered in the previous section — is non-parametric and captures nonlinear relationships, making it more general but slower to compute than the parametric tests.
Automated Feature Selection with scikit-learn Pipelines

Wrapping feature selection inside a scikit-learn Pipeline ensures that the selector is re-fit on each training fold during cross-validation, preventing data leakage. This is the correct way to evaluate feature selection in conjunction with a downstream model.

from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFromModel, RFECV
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, GridSearchCV
import numpy as np

# Pipeline: scale → select → classify
# SelectFromModel refits its internal estimator on each training fold
pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("scaler",    StandardScaler()),
    ("selector",  SelectFromModel(
        RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=50, random_state=42),
        threshold="median"        # keep features with importance > median
    )),
    ("classifier", RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)),
])

# Cross-validate the whole pipeline — no leakage
scores = cross_val_score(pipeline, X, y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc")
print(f"Pipeline AUC: {scores.mean():.4f} ± {scores.std():.4f}")

# Grid search over threshold
param_grid = {
    "selector__threshold": ["mean", "median", "0.1*mean"],
    "selector__estimator__n_estimators": [50, 100],
}
grid = GridSearchCV(pipeline, param_grid, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc", n_jobs=-1)
grid.fit(X, y)
print(f"Best threshold: {grid.best_params_['selector__threshold']}")
print(f"Best CV AUC:    {grid.best_score_:.4f}")

# Inspect which features were selected in the final fitted pipeline
grid.best_estimator_.fit(X, y)
selector = grid.best_estimator_.named_steps["selector"]
selected_mask = selector.get_support()
print(f"Features selected: {selected_mask.sum()} / {len(selected_mask)}")
Small d, optimal subset for specific model

When the number of features vastly exceeds the number of samples (e.g., 20,000 gene expression features with 200 samples), standard wrapper methods are computationally infeasible and statistically unreliable. Recommended approach: start with a strict filter (t-test per feature, Bonferroni-corrected threshold), then apply Elastic Net (L1+L2 combined) rather than pure Lasso, which is more stable when features are correlated.

from sklearn.linear_model import ElasticNetCV
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectFpr, f_classif
import numpy as np

# Simulate genomics: 200 samples, 5000 genes
n_samples, n_genes = 200, 5000
np.random.seed(42)
X_genes = np.random.randn(n_samples, n_genes)
# 20 truly informative genes
informative = np.random.choice(n_genes, 20, replace=False)
y_genes = (X_genes[:, informative[:10]].mean(axis=1) > 0).astype(int)

# Step 1: Statistical filter with FDR correction
selector = SelectFpr(f_classif, alpha=0.05)   # Bonferroni/FDR correction
X_filtered = selector.fit_transform(X_genes, y_genes)
print(f"After FDR filter: {X_filtered.shape[1]} genes (from {n_genes})")

# Step 2: Elastic Net for sparse + stable selection
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegressionCV
elastic = LogisticRegressionCV(
    Cs=10, penalty="elasticnet", solver="saga",
    l1_ratios=[0.5, 0.7, 0.9, 1.0],
    cv=5, max_iter=5000, random_state=42
)
elastic.fit(X_filtered, y_genes)
n_selected = (elastic.coef_[0] != 0).sum()
print(f"Elastic Net selected: {n_selected} genes")

Filter methods score features independently using statistical measures of association with the target variable. For a continuous target and continuous feature, the Pearson correlation coefficient is the standard measure:
For categorical targets, mutual information measures how much knowing a feature reduces uncertainty about the class label:

attrs.latex: I(X; Y) = \sum_{x \in X} \sum_{y \in Y} P(x, y) \log \frac{P(x, y)}{P(x) P(y)}

content.text: Wrapper methods use model performance as their scoring criterion. Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) removes the least important feature at each step, training the model on the reduced set each time:
Start with all d features. Train model, get feature importances w₁, …, w_d.
Remove the feature with the lowest importance score.
Re-train on the remaining d−1 features. Repeat until the desired number of features remains.
Select the subset that maximised cross-validation performance during elimination.

Embedded methods leverage the model's own regularisation. L1 (Lasso) regression adds a penalty proportional to the absolute value of coefficients, which drives irrelevant coefficients exactly to zero:

The L1 penalty creates a sparse solution: features whose coefficients are zeroed out are effectively excluded. The regularisation strength λ is a hyperparameter that controls how aggressively features are removed — larger λ produces fewer selected features.
Real-World Example: Credit Risk Feature Selection

A credit scoring model might start with 200 raw features (payment history, balances, account ages, inquiry counts, etc.). Feature selection reduces this to the 20–30 most predictive variables, improving both model performance and regulatory explainability — regulations like GDPR require that automated decisions can be explained in terms of the input variables used.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV
from sklearn.feature_selection import (
    SelectKBest, mutual_info_classif,
    RFE, SelectFromModel
)
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Simulate credit scoring dataset: 1000 samples, 50 features, 10 informative
X, y = make_classification(
    n_samples=1000, n_features=50, n_informative=10,
    n_redundant=5, n_repeated=0, random_state=42
)
feature_names = [f"feat_{i}" for i in range(50)]

# ── Step 1: Filter — mutual information ──
selector_filter = SelectKBest(mutual_info_classif, k=20)
X_filter = selector_filter.fit_transform(X, y)
mi_scores = selector_filter.scores_
top_mi = np.argsort(mi_scores)[-10:][::-1]
print("Top 10 by mutual information:", [feature_names[i] for i in top_mi])

# ── Step 2: Embedded — Lasso (L1 regularised logistic regression) ──
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegressionCV
lasso_model = LogisticRegressionCV(
    Cs=10, penalty="l1", solver="saga",
    cv=5, max_iter=5000, random_state=42
)
lasso_model.fit(X, y)
nonzero_coefs = np.where(lasso_model.coef_[0] != 0)[0]
print(f"Lasso selected {len(nonzero_coefs)} features: {[feature_names[i] for i in nonzero_coefs]}")

# ── Step 3: Wrapper — RFE with Random Forest ──
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rfe = RFE(estimator=rf, n_features_to_select=15, step=1)
rfe.fit(X, y)
rfe_selected = np.where(rfe.support_)[0]
print(f"RFE selected: {[feature_names[i] for i in rfe_selected]}")

# ── Compare model performance ──
rf_all   = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rf_rfe   = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)

score_all = cross_val_score(rf_all, X, y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc").mean()
score_rfe = cross_val_score(rf_rfe, X[:, rfe_selected], y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc").mean()

print(f"AUC with all 50 features: {score_all:.4f}")
print(f"AUC with 15 RFE features: {score_rfe:.4f}")
# Often the reduced model matches or exceeds the full model due to less noise
Mistake 1: Feature selection before train-test split (data leakage)

# WRONG — selects features using the entire dataset, including test data
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=10)
X_selected = selector.fit_transform(X, y)              # sees all y labels!
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X_selected, y)

# RIGHT — select features only on training data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)
selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=10)
X_train_sel = selector.fit_transform(X_train, y_train)   # fit on train only
X_test_sel  = selector.transform(X_test)                  # apply to test
Mistake 2: Removing correlated features without checking which to keep

# WRONG — arbitrarily removes the first of each correlated pair
corr_matrix = pd.DataFrame(X).corr().abs()
upper = corr_matrix.where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
to_drop = [col for col in upper.columns if any(upper[col] > 0.9)]
# This ignores which of the two correlated features is more predictive

# RIGHT — keep the one with higher correlation to target
import pandas as pd, numpy as np
df = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=feature_names)
df["target"] = y
corr_with_target = df.corr()["target"].abs()
corr_matrix = df.drop("target", axis=1).corr().abs()
# Remove the feature with LOWER target correlation from each high-correlation pair
upper = corr_matrix.where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
to_drop = []
for col in upper.columns:
    for row in upper.index:
        if upper.loc[row, col] > 0.9:
            # Drop the one with lower target correlation
            if corr_with_target[col] < corr_with_target[row]:
                to_drop.append(col)
            else:
                to_drop.append(row)
to_drop = list(set(to_drop))
print("Dropping:", to_drop)

Filter methods run in O(n·d) time (n samples, d features) and are independent of the downstream model, making them suitable for datasets with thousands of features. Mutual information estimation for continuous features requires density estimation (or binning), which adds an O(n log n) term per feature but is still fast in absolute terms.

Wrapper methods are the bottleneck: RFE trains the model O(d) times, so total cost is O(d × model training time). For a dataset with 1000 features and a training time of 10 seconds per model, RFE takes 2.8 hours. Practical workarounds include: using a faster surrogate model (e.g., a shallow decision tree) for elimination and then retraining the full model on the selected features, or using step>1 to eliminate multiple features per round.

Embedded methods (Lasso, tree importance) add negligible overhead to a single model training run, making them the most scalable option for high-dimensional data. Random Forest feature importance is computed for free during training, with no additional computation cost.
— The standard library. Provides SelectKBest, SelectPercentile, RFE, RFECV, SelectFromModel, VarianceThreshold, chi2, f_classif, mutual_info_classif, and more.
— Model-agnostic feature importance using Shapley values. More reliable than tree-based importance for correlated features, but slower (O(n × features × samples)).
— A wrapper method that compares each feature against randomly permuted shadow features. More principled than RFE for determining which features are genuinely informative.
— Automated feature selection pipeline that combines correlation analysis, target correlation, and XGBoost importance with recursive elimination.

# SHAP-based feature importance (model-agnostic)
import shap
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier

model = GradientBoostingClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)

explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_train)

# Mean absolute SHAP value per feature
mean_shap = np.abs(shap_values).mean(axis=0)
top_features = np.argsort(mean_shap)[-10:][::-1]
print("Top 10 SHAP features:", [feature_names[i] for i in top_features])

# Plot (requires matplotlib)
# shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_train, feature_names=feature_names)

Feature selection decisions made on one dataset may not generalise to another. A common reliability failure is selecting features that are spuriously correlated with the label in the training sample due to sampling noise — particularly when the number of features d exceeds the number of samples n. This is the multiple comparisons problem: if you test 1000 features for correlation with a random label at p < 0.05, you expect 50 false positives.

Mitigation strategies: use cross-validated feature selection (RFECV rather than RFE) so that the selected set is stable across folds; apply Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg correction when using statistical tests; and test the selected feature set on a fully held-out test set that was never used during any stage of selection. Adversarial inputs that manipulate feature values to cross selection thresholds are a concern in high-stakes settings such as fraud detection — features selected purely on historical data may be gamed by adversaries who understand the model.

Filter methods score features independently using statistical measures of association with the target variable. For a continuous target and continuous feature, the Pearson correlation coefficient is the standard measure:
For categorical targets, mutual information measures how much knowing a feature reduces uncertainty about the class label:

attrs.latex: I(X; Y) = \sum_{x \in X} \sum_{y \in Y} P(x, y) \log \frac{P(x, y)}{P(x) P(y)}

content.text: Wrapper methods use model performance as their scoring criterion. Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) removes the least important feature at each step, training the model on the reduced set each time:
Start with all d features. Train model, get feature importances w₁, …, w_d.
Remove the feature with the lowest importance score.
Re-train on the remaining d−1 features. Repeat until the desired number of features remains.
Select the subset that maximised cross-validation performance during elimination.

Embedded methods leverage the model's own regularisation. L1 (Lasso) regression adds a penalty proportional to the absolute value of coefficients, which drives irrelevant coefficients exactly to zero:

The L1 penalty creates a sparse solution: features whose coefficients are zeroed out are effectively excluded. The regularisation strength λ is a hyperparameter that controls how aggressively features are removed — larger λ produces fewer selected features.
Real-World Example: Credit Risk Feature Selection

A credit scoring model might start with 200 raw features (payment history, balances, account ages, inquiry counts, etc.). Feature selection reduces this to the 20–30 most predictive variables, improving both model performance and regulatory explainability — regulations like GDPR require that automated decisions can be explained in terms of the input variables used.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.linear_model import LassoCV
from sklearn.feature_selection import (
    SelectKBest, mutual_info_classif,
    RFE, SelectFromModel
)
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# Simulate credit scoring dataset: 1000 samples, 50 features, 10 informative
X, y = make_classification(
    n_samples=1000, n_features=50, n_informative=10,
    n_redundant=5, n_repeated=0, random_state=42
)
feature_names = [f"feat_{i}" for i in range(50)]

# ── Step 1: Filter — mutual information ──
selector_filter = SelectKBest(mutual_info_classif, k=20)
X_filter = selector_filter.fit_transform(X, y)
mi_scores = selector_filter.scores_
top_mi = np.argsort(mi_scores)[-10:][::-1]
print("Top 10 by mutual information:", [feature_names[i] for i in top_mi])

# ── Step 2: Embedded — Lasso (L1 regularised logistic regression) ──
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegressionCV
lasso_model = LogisticRegressionCV(
    Cs=10, penalty="l1", solver="saga",
    cv=5, max_iter=5000, random_state=42
)
lasso_model.fit(X, y)
nonzero_coefs = np.where(lasso_model.coef_[0] != 0)[0]
print(f"Lasso selected {len(nonzero_coefs)} features: {[feature_names[i] for i in nonzero_coefs]}")

# ── Step 3: Wrapper — RFE with Random Forest ──
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rfe = RFE(estimator=rf, n_features_to_select=15, step=1)
rfe.fit(X, y)
rfe_selected = np.where(rfe.support_)[0]
print(f"RFE selected: {[feature_names[i] for i in rfe_selected]}")

# ── Compare model performance ──
rf_all   = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rf_rfe   = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)

score_all = cross_val_score(rf_all, X, y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc").mean()
score_rfe = cross_val_score(rf_rfe, X[:, rfe_selected], y, cv=5, scoring="roc_auc").mean()

print(f"AUC with all 50 features: {score_all:.4f}")
print(f"AUC with 15 RFE features: {score_rfe:.4f}")
# Often the reduced model matches or exceeds the full model due to less noise
Mistake 1: Feature selection before train-test split (data leakage)

# WRONG — selects features using the entire dataset, including test data
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=10)
X_selected = selector.fit_transform(X, y)              # sees all y labels!
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X_selected, y)

# RIGHT — select features only on training data
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2)
selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=10)
X_train_sel = selector.fit_transform(X_train, y_train)   # fit on train only
X_test_sel  = selector.transform(X_test)                  # apply to test
Mistake 2: Removing correlated features without checking which to keep

# WRONG — arbitrarily removes the first of each correlated pair
corr_matrix = pd.DataFrame(X).corr().abs()
upper = corr_matrix.where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
to_drop = [col for col in upper.columns if any(upper[col] > 0.9)]
# This ignores which of the two correlated features is more predictive

# RIGHT — keep the one with higher correlation to target
import pandas as pd, numpy as np
df = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=feature_names)
df["target"] = y
corr_with_target = df.corr()["target"].abs()
corr_matrix = df.drop("target", axis=1).corr().abs()
# Remove the feature with LOWER target correlation from each high-correlation pair
upper = corr_matrix.where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool))
to_drop = []
for col in upper.columns:
    for row in upper.index:
        if upper.loc[row, col] > 0.9:
            # Drop the one with lower target correlation
            if corr_with_target[col] < corr_with_target[row]:
                to_drop.append(col)
            else:
                to_drop.append(row)
to_drop = list(set(to_drop))
print("Dropping:", to_drop)

Filter methods run in O(n·d) time (n samples, d features) and are independent of the downstream model, making them suitable for datasets with thousands of features. Mutual information estimation for continuous features requires density estimation (or binning), which adds an O(n log n) term per feature but is still fast in absolute terms.

Wrapper methods are the bottleneck: RFE trains the model O(d) times, so total cost is O(d × model training time). For a dataset with 1000 features and a training time of 10 seconds per model, RFE takes 2.8 hours. Practical workarounds include: using a faster surrogate model (e.g., a shallow decision tree) for elimination and then retraining the full model on the selected features, or using step>1 to eliminate multiple features per round.

Embedded methods (Lasso, tree importance) add negligible overhead to a single model training run, making them the most scalable option for high-dimensional data. Random Forest feature importance is computed for free during training, with no additional computation cost.
— The standard library. Provides SelectKBest, SelectPercentile, RFE, RFECV, SelectFromModel, VarianceThreshold, chi2, f_classif, mutual_info_classif, and more.
— Model-agnostic feature importance using Shapley values. More reliable than tree-based importance for correlated features, but slower (O(n × features × samples)).
— A wrapper method that compares each feature against randomly permuted shadow features. More principled than RFE for determining which features are genuinely informative.
— Automated feature selection pipeline that combines correlation analysis, target correlation, and XGBoost importance with recursive elimination.

# Mean absolute SHAP value per feature

top_features = np.argsort(mean_shap)[-10:][::-1]
print("Top 10 SHAP features:", [feature_names[i] for i in top_features])

Feature selection decisions made on one dataset may not generalise to another. A common reliability failure is selecting features that are spuriously correlated with the label in the training sample due to sampling noise — particularly when the number of features d exceeds the number of samples n. This is the multiple comparisons problem: if you test 1000 features for correlation with a random label at p < 0.05, you expect 50 false positives.

Mitigation strategies: use cross-validated feature selection (RFECV rather than RFE) so that the selected set is stable across folds; apply Bonferroni or Benjamini-Hochberg correction when using statistical tests; and test the selected feature set on a fully held-out test set that was never used during any stage of selection. : Statistical scoring (correlation, chi-square, ANOVA); fast but ignores interactions -
: Model performance-based (RFE, forward/backward selection); accurate but slow -

(Random Forest, Lasso) → validate with cross-validation.
- Use feature selection when many irrelevant features, high-dimensional data, need interpretability, or overfitting; use feature extraction for very high-dimensional (images/text) or correlated features.
